# Questions in Livestream 217

Live Stream #217: Cringe Scholars Society... with Red Wine

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=643" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:43</a> | Which decade has been the best in your experience, and which has been the worst? | He says the early 2000s were probably his best period, while the 1990s were pretty miserable for him. | He explains that the 1990s were dominated by undergraduate work and data-center work, while roughly 1995-2005 brought major positive changes: marriage, starting businesses, and beginning seminary. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=786" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:06</a> | Is there a science-fiction work that especially influenced your writing? | Yes. He names Babylon 5 as especially influential, while also mentioning earlier influences such as Star Trek and Doctor Who. | He says Star Trek shaped him as a child and Doctor Who influenced his intellectual interests, but Babylon 5 most shaped his writing because it used long character arcs and treated humanity as flawed, limited, and not at the top of the universe. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=1022" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:02</a> | How did Doctor Who inspire you to pursue higher education? | He says it stirred his imagination and intellectual curiosity in a way that helped push him toward serious study. | He says the Doctor embodied broad learning: history, electronics, making and repairing things, and wide-ranging knowledge, which made higher education seem like a way to become that sort of person. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=1136" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:56</a> | Based on Scott Stripling's Jericho comments, is he basically adopting Brian Windle's view? | Yes. He says Stripling is essentially in that camp. | He says Stripling appears to have adopted Windle's Jericho view, including a Septuagint-oriented reading of 1 Kings 6:1, but argues the view mishandles late Bronze II evidence and treats pottery as post-destruction resettlement in a way he finds implausible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=1451" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:11</a> | Before the chariot, did Egyptians use non-military wheeled vehicles for transportation? | No. He says they did not normally use wheeled transport that way before chariots. | He says the wheel enters Egypt with the chariot around the 15th dynasty; before that, Egyptian scenes show pack animals such as donkeys rather than wagons, carts, or other wheeled vehicles. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=1897" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:37</a> | As a writer, is it reasonable to feel guilty for what your characters suffer? | Yes, to a point. He says your characters should move you, though you still have to let the story do hard things. | He says writers should have emotional reactions to their characters because readers likely will too; guilt or grief over what happens can be normal, but properly written characters still act according to the world and story. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=2096" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:56</a> | How should I understand Leviticus 5:15-19 on trespass? Is it really about sustaining ignorance? | He says the passage is about objective guilt and required restitution, not about preserving ignorance as a virtue. | He frames trespass as one tier among iniquity, trespass, and rebellion: not feigned ignorance, but accidentally crossing ritual boundaries, such as a priest eating a reserved portion, after which atonement, restitution, and forgiveness are provided. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=2476" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:16</a> | Is it improper to picture sin offerings like a barbecue where nobody gets to eat? | He says no: barbecue is not a good analogy because these offerings involve incineration and ash rather than shared cooked meat. | He rejects the barbecue image because the offering is not cooked meat for eating; the point is burning or reducing the offering to ash, which is unlike even heavily charred barbecue. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=2571" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">42:51</a> | What is your favorite scholarly commentary series on the Bible, and why? | He does not name one favorite; he likes NICOT/NICNT, Hermeneia, and Word, while stressing that commentaries have to be judged book by book. | He says he chooses commentaries case by case, often starting with NICOT/NICNT; he also values Hermeneia as a strong liberal series and Word Biblical Commentary, especially for New Testament, while noting no series covers every book equally well. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=2717" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:17</a> | How did the Hebrews celebrate birthdays, if they celebrated them at all? | He says there is little evidence for normal Israelite birthday celebration, unlike some surrounding cultures. | He says people knew birthdays for age reckoning, but ordinary birthday celebration, especially for children or adults, is largely a much later custom associated with European royal practice rather than ancient Hebrews. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=2802" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:42</a> | Would people in that world even know their exact date of birth? | Yes. He says calendars, ritual cycles, and military conscription made exact dates and ages important enough to track. | He argues that age mattered for military eligibility and that ritual life required tracking weeks, months, new moons, Sabbaths, and festivals, so communities knew the day and could mark when someone was born. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=2985" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:45</a> | Did the Jewish calendar make birthdays drift from year to year? | Yes, lunar-solar reckoning could complicate strict anniversary matching. | He says lunar calendars, especially before intercalation, could move the seasonal placement of a date, but the same calendar date still functioned socially; imperfect calendars were still usable in daily life. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=3133" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:13</a> | In Psalm 1:6, what does "the way of the righteous" mean? | He says it refers to the whole life-path or mode of conduct of the righteous, set over against the path of the wicked. | He reads Psalm 1:6 as a contrastive couplet: the Lord intimately knows the righteous life-pattern, while the wicked way perishes, so "way" means lifestyle, path, or how people live. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=3317" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:17</a> | Should psalms be read separately, or in the context of neighboring passages? | Both. He says individual psalms stand on their own, but the Psalter was also arranged by theme, so context matters. | He says each psalm is an individual composition, but the Psalter was collected and grouped by common themes, such as the Psalms of Ascent, so neighboring context can matter without making one psalm simply read into another. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=3520" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:40</a> | Do you know any good resources on how Jews used the Psalms liturgically or from festival to festival? | He recalls that such resources exist, but he cannot confidently name one on the spot. | He remembers encountering a book on the background and liturgical use of the Psalms but cannot name it; he also clarifies that Malachi is later than Psalm 1, so similarities are intertextual dialogue rather than Psalm 1 rebutting Malachi. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=3653" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:53</a> | Will there be another book stream? | Yes. He expects another one. | He says there will be a stream for All the Stars as Angels about a month after release, likely on a Wednesday, and that it will allow questions on both that book and Reliquary of the Dead because they belong to the same series. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=3772" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:52</a> | Joel 3:4-6 says Phoenicians and Philistines sold Jewish captives to Greeks. What historical period does that fit? | He places it broadly in the first-millennium context where those coastal trading powers and Greek markets overlap. | He says Joel is difficult to date because Greeks, Phoenicians, and Israelites had contact well before the Hellenistic period; he allows anything roughly from the ninth to fifth centuries BC and says the passage alone cannot narrow it further. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=4011" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:51</a> | What are your thoughts on the new perspective on Paul? | He finds parts of it useful, but rejects versions that make Paul's Christianity merely a later invention or flatten the gospel into covenant sociology. | He accepts the covenant-community emphasis as a useful part of the new perspective, but rejects extensions that claim Paul invented Christianity from Jesus or that detach Paul from what Christ preached. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=4157" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:17</a> | When will the sequel to Reliquary of the Dead be available? | He says it will be available in four days and notes that a third book is also underway. | He says the sequel is due in four days, some people already have copies, and the series will continue because a third book is nearly complete, with ideas also existing for later books. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=4215" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:15</a> | Given that Nineveh is landlocked, how long would it take Jonah to get from the coast near Aleppo to Nineveh? | He says about a week, maybe two if delayed, since that route was not especially long by ancient standards. | He says Aleppo to Nineveh was not far by ancient travel standards; with provisions or a caravan, a person could plausibly make the trip in about a week, or two if moving slowly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=4340" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:20</a> | What do you think the fish in Jonah was? | He does not identify a precise species and is content to leave it at a large fish rather than over-specifying it. | He personally thinks it was probably a whale or large cetacean, but is not dogmatic; he notes Israelites could call any great sea creature Leviathan-like and that modern reports of whales swallowing humans make the broad idea possible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=4417" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:37</a> | Was Jonah intended as satire or as a real story? | He says it was meant as a real story. | He argues Jonah concerns a real person known from Kings, so he does not treat the book as satire, while still acknowledging that the event is extraordinary. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=4471" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:31</a> | Did Jonah die and come back to life after being swallowed? | He leans yes, treating Jonah as having died and then been restored. | He says Jonah would not naturally survive in a whale because of oxygen deprivation and the hostile stomach environment; he treats the story as resurrection, tied to Jesus' sign of Jonah and God's power over death. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=4904" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:44</a> | Any more movement on an audiobook? | Yes, but with a snag. Some chapters were recorded, though the first ones may need re-recording or repair. | He says recording has happened, but the first chapters did not turn out well, so he has to decide whether to edit them or re-record while balancing other projects. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=5108" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:08</a> | How much would you need to charge to make audiobook costs back? | He says audiobook production is extremely expensive, with market pricing around twenty dollars and little realistic profit margin. | He says professional audiobook production can run hundreds of dollars per finalized hour and a ten-hour book is costly, while royalties and normal consumer pricing leave little room to recover costs quickly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=5244" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:24</a> | Any thoughts on T. C. Smith's Josephus and Jesus? | He has not read it, so he declines to comment. | He says he generally does not comment on books he has not read or at least examined, so he cannot responsibly evaluate Josephus and Jesus. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=5294" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:28:14</a> | What did Jerome mean when he said Jews had excised Tobit from the catalog of divine Scriptures and moved it to the Apocrypha? | He says Jerome was reflecting his preference for the Jewish canon and treating Tobit as excluded from Scripture rather than properly canonical. | He explains that Jerome highly valued the Jewish canon, knew Greek, Hebrew, and Latin, rejected the Apocrypha as Scripture, and translated some apocryphal works mainly because others wanted them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=5387" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:47</a> | If Tobit could be excised, does that mean it had first been part of Jewish Scripture? | Not necessarily. He says canon formation worked more as a process of elimination from a broad pool of texts than as a simple add-then-remove sequence. | He compares Old Testament canon formation to winnowing: many scrolls were provisionally considered, then excluded by criteria such as consistency with Torah, known authorship, and presentation to the temple, so Tobit could be ruled out without having been finally accepted. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=5758" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:58</a> | How reliable is Papias? | It depends on the topic. He says Papias is early and interesting, but uneven like other church fathers. | He calls Papias an interesting early father, possibly a disciple of John, but says his reliability varies by subject just as with other church fathers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=5811" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:51</a> | What do you think of Scott Stripling's Shiloh dig? | He praises the fieldwork and organization, but says the reporting is often badly biased and distorts what was found. | He says ABR and Stripling are excellent at fundraising, organizing digs, and field technique, but he strongly criticizes their reports as ideologically driven and prone to distorting the interpretation of finds. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=5922" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:42</a> | Can we still glean anything useful from ABR's dig even if they distort things? | Some useful artifacts were found, but he says contextual distortion makes the interpretation much harder to trust. | He says Shiloh was underexplored and ABR found interesting items such as gold foil and horned altars, but he would not trust their pottery typology or contextual claims because ideological commitments color the reporting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=6165" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:45</a> | After getting a THM, what should I be doing now if I want to teach at a university? | He says to keep building toward a PhD, produce research early, and have backup plans because the field is brutally competitive. | He says university teaching is highly competitive and political, so the questioner should make it plan A while also having fallback plans, begin producing research, identify possible doctoral advisors, read their work, and meet scholars at conferences. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=6383" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:46:23</a> | What is even the process for getting hired into one of those university jobs? | Usually PhD, then postdoc or fellowship, then more publication and networking before competing for a very small number of jobs. | He describes a path of finishing a PhD, securing a postdoc or fellowship if possible, producing peer-reviewed work, networking, and then competing against many qualified applicants in a very crowded market. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=6599" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:59</a> | Should I only go for Bible-college jobs, or secular ones as well? | He says go for both: secular schools care more about research, while Bible colleges care more about doctrine and denominational fit. | He says there is no harm in applying to both, but the criteria differ: secular institutions emphasize research output, while Bible colleges scrutinize denominational and doctrinal alignment more closely. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=6803" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:23</a> | Will the video game be your main focus after the books are done? | Yes, at least for the next couple of months. | He says the game will likely be his focus for at least the next two months, then uses the surrounding discussion to stress that writing and game development require consistent daily work rather than waiting for inspiration. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=7332" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:02:12</a> | Do some stories only work in certain media, and how do you choose the best medium? | Yes. He says the right medium depends on the type of story and on the medium the writer is actually equipped to write well. | He says stories with many possible outcomes may fit games, compressed emotional pieces may fit poetry, and archaeology-heavy narratives may fit books or film; writers should choose the medium they can actually work in and design stories for its limits. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=7616" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:56</a> | What do you think of The X-Files? | He likes it as paranormal fiction, though he says it can be disturbing and does not really recommend it universally. | He says he enjoys paranormal fiction and liked the first seasons of The X-Files, but notes that parts are disturbing, the show is not something he broadly recommends, and the series eventually petered out. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=7979" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:59</a> | How would you explain Genesis 5:24 under your long-ages or clan-style view? | He treats Enoch's case as compatible with that framework, without seeing it as a decisive problem for the view. | He says Enoch can be understood as the key figure or famous chief after whom a clan is named; the verse about God taking Enoch refers to that individual within the broader clan-progenitor framework. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=8116" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:16</a> | What can we say about the eternal destiny of someone who seemed to be turning toward Jesus but died before making a clear faith commitment? | He says we do not know for certain, but there is at least ground for hope and the matter belongs to the Lord's judgment. | He says the person's destiny belongs to the Lord, and because the person had not clearly accepted or rejected Christ, there may be room for hope, possibly under a second-chance view, but Scripture does not give certainty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=8297" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:17</a> | Was Terah Abraham's biological father rather than a clan progenitor? | He says it is hard to know for sure and does not think the text forces one answer. | He says Terah may have been Abraham's father, grandfather, or more distant ancestor because Hebrew kinship terms are broad; in any case Terah remains a clan progenitor, and Abraham's line splits off while Terah's clan continues in Haran. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=8553" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:33</a> | What is your favorite whiskey? | He names his favorite directly and answers as a matter of taste rather than scholarship. | He jokes that homemade is his favorite, then names Laphroaig as his favorite brand because he likes peaty Islay whiskies, while saying he dislikes bourbon and Jack Daniel's because of the corn-mash flavor. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=8776" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:16</a> | Have you been to Russia and tried vodka? | He answers personally and says he has not had that experience in the way the question suggests. | He says he has never been to Russia, though he has tried many kinds of vodka, then moves into a discussion of cognac and homemade spirits. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=8892" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:28:12</a> | What is cognac? | He says it is essentially a type of brandy. | He explains cognac as distilled wine aged in oak: make wine, distill it into brandy, then age it; he adds that aging dramatically improves it and that related fortified drinks involve adding wine back in. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=9019" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:19</a> | I'm visiting Toronto for the first time. Are there any restaurants I should go to? | He gives a few remembered recommendations, while admitting his memory may be partial. | He recommends downtown Chinatown and dim sum in particular, says Toronto has many excellent and authentic restaurants, and suggests local reviews are more trustworthy there than in Vancouver. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=9466" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:46</a> | Same question, but how should we think about Noah and his sons as clan progenitors? | He says the same clan-progenitor logic can apply there too. | He applies the same model: Noah has his clan, and each son has a clan as well, so the genealogical logic can involve family lines and clan identities rather than only isolated individuals. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=9610" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:10</a> | Are Joseph and Daniel the only Old Testament figures with no recorded sin? Do you agree? | He does not fully agree: Joseph's pride seems implied in the narrative, while Daniel is less clearly shown that way. | He says Joseph appears proud and favored by Jacob, even if the narrative does not explicitly label it sin, whereas Daniel is taken from home into exile and the text does not present the same kind of implied fault. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=9850" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:10</a> | What does the "burning coals" image mean in Romans 12:20? | He explains it with the ancient image of carrying a brazier on the head, tied to repentance, distress, or a plea for help. | He says the image alludes to a public act of penance involving a pan or brazier of hot coals held over the head, so doing good to an enemy increases the moral pressure toward contrition and repentance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=10150" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:10</a> | Have you ever played Pyramid: Challenge of the Pharaoh's Dream, and if so how accurate is it? | No. He never played it, so he cannot assess its historical accuracy. | He says he never played Pyramid: Challenge of the Pharaoh's Dream; in 1996 he was more interested in the Ultima series, so he cannot evaluate the game's historical accuracy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=10245" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:50:45</a> | Do you believe Daniel was a castrated eunuch? | He says Daniel was made a eunuch, but probably not by surgical castration in the usual sense. | He says Daniel was made a eunuch, but distinguishes that from cutting castration, arguing the ancient procedure was more likely crushing and then leaves the details there. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=10352" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:32</a> | How long have you been doing keto? | He answers from personal experience and says it has been several years. | He says he and his household have done keto for about four or five years, motivated by concerns about diabetes, blood sugar, insulin, and eyesight, while acknowledging they sometimes slip and return to it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=11332" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:08:52</a> | Is goat's milk and sheep's milk also a no-go for lactose-intolerant people? | Yes, he says those can still be a problem. | He answers yes, then the discussion turns to diet details: cream, butter, olive oil, curries, and other keto staples can be useful, but dairy tolerance and additives such as carrageenan may still matter. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=11966" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:19:26</a> | What is the second-best Ultima after Exodus? | He picks Ultima: Pagan as his runner-up. | He names Ultima VIII: Pagan as his favorite after Exodus, while praising Exodus for clever CGA color tricks and also noting he enjoyed Ultima VII and the Underworld games. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=12232" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:52</a> | How excited are you about AI helping read the Herculaneum scrolls? | Cautiously interested. He wants publications and readable data more than publicity. | He says the hard imaging work has long involved X-ray tomography, while AI can help reassemble and flatten the scroll data; he will be more excited when the texts are published in readable form. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=12370" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:26:10</a> | Are Solomon's 700 wives and 300 concubines literal, symbolic, or exaggerated? | He thinks the numbers may be rounded, but still reflect a genuinely huge treaty-marriage system. | He explains the wives as treaty exchanges tied to Solomon's control of trade routes; the figures may be rounded, but they are broadly literal, and repeated royal successions could require renewed marriages with the same regions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=13213" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:40:13</a> | Is there any update on the Amorite Akkadian cuneiform tablet from 2023, and are there other biblical ramifications? | No major update since publication. He says its main significance is how strongly it supports Amoritic as Hebrew's closest cognate and thus illuminates Israel's Amorite background. | He says the 2023 publication remains the latest, and the main implication is linguistic and historical: Amoritic appears very close to Hebrew, which fits biblical references to Israel's Amorite connections and explains why Israelite DNA could resemble Canaanite populations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=13450" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:44:10</a> | What do you think of the idea that only Levites came from outside Israel while the other tribes were Canaanites? | He rejects that small-exodus view and says it depends too heavily on documentary-hypothesis assumptions while failing to explain the conquest adequately. | He identifies the view with Richard Elliott Friedman's Levitical Exodus theory and rejects it because it depends on documentary-hypothesis assumptions, cannot explain conquering major Amorite cities, and ignores non-Levite Egyptian names such as Hur. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=14098" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:54:58</a> | Do you have advice on presenting scholarly work so lay people can understand it? | Yes: translate jargon, use simple words and sentences, and write at a very accessible reading level. | He advises translating scholarly jargon into simple language, writing at about a grade-school to teenage comprehension level, making careful distinctions, and using clear prose rather than relying on technical vocabulary. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=14872" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:07:52</a> | What do you think about the Manoppello image and its match to the shroud? | He sees it as one devotional artifact among several similar Christ-image traditions, shaped by familiar Byzantine iconographic patterns rather than as unique proof of authenticity. | He says the Manoppello image belongs among multiple devotional artifacts circulating in Western Europe, many connected to Eastern Orthodox contexts, and that its visual model resembles common Christ Pantocrator patterns in Byzantine iconography. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=15057" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:10:57</a> | Why are apologists usually worse at defending the Old Testament than the New Testament? | He says the Old Testament takes far more background knowledge, languages, and history to master, so many apologists never develop the depth it requires. | He says the Old Testament is much larger, spans about a millennium, and requires Egyptian, Levantine, Assyrian, Hittite, Phoenician, Philistine, Amorite, Moabite, and language knowledge, while apologetics programs often emphasize church history, philosophy, and theology over original languages. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/4fDBgW37xto?t=15453" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:17:33</a> | Is it possible to write a horror story with a good Christian message? | Yes. He says horror can carry Christian themes well when the world, contrast, and moral stakes are built carefully. | He says a good story needs world, character, and theme, and horror can carry Christian meaning through contrast, such as a haunted-house story where light overcomes darkness and the character reaches a moral or spiritual pivot. |
